sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 60, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - The green 2x2 dot (id=34) is the player/agent that moves with arrow keys - It started inside a gray box at bottom-left and moved RIGHT by 2 columns - The gray box seems to be a starting container - Right side has two cross-shaped targets (red top, blue bottom) - Divider at col 32-33 with white dots Goal hypothesis: Navigate the green dot from left side to one of the right-side targets
- Plan: Try moving DOWN to exit box from bottom, then navigate to divider
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 59 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the green dot has moved DOWN from [30,20]-[31,21] to [36,20]-[37,21] (or similar). It exited the dark red box and is now below it.
Let me check the segmentation to see where the green dot is now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check green dot position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        r1, c1 = min(p[0] for p in b), min(p[1] for p in b)
        r2, c2 = max(p[0] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Green dot: id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{r1},{c1}]-[{r2},{c2}]")

# Check all left-side objects
print("\n=== Left-side objects ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    r1, c1 = min(p[0] for p in b), min(p[1] for p in b)
    r2, c2 = max(p[0] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
    if c2 < 32 and n['pixels'] > 4:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{r1},{c1}]-[{r2},{c2}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The green dot is at [32,20]-[33,21], which is the same position as before the DOWN actions. Wait, that's strange. The green dot was at [30,20]-[31,21] before the DOWN actions, and now it's at [32,20]-[33,21]. So it moved DOWN by 2 rows (1 DOWN action worth of movement).
But I sent 3 DOWN actions. The green dot only moved 2 rows, which means it's blocked after moving 2 rows.
The dark red box is at [30,18]-[33,21], which spans rows 30-33. The green dot is at [32,20]-[33,21], which is at the bottom of the dark red box. The green dot can't move further DOWN because it's blocked by the box boundary.
Wait, but the green dot is at [32,20]-[33,21], and the dark red box is at [30,18]-[33,21]. The green dot is still inside the dark red box. The box spans rows 30-33, so the green dot at rows 32-33 is at the bottom of the box.
Hmm, the green dot can't exit the dark red box from any side. It's trapped inside the box.
Wait, but earlier the green dot was able to move from [42,8]-[43,9] (inside the gray box at [38,8]-[43,13]) to [32,8]-[33,9] (outside the gray box). So the green dot can exit containers.
But now the green dot is trapped inside the dark red box at [30,18]-[33,21]. The box spans rows 30-33, cols 18-21. The green dot is at [32,20]-[33,21], which is at the bottom-right of the box.
Maybe the green dot can only exit containers from certain sides, or maybe the dark red box is different from the gray box.
Let me try moving the green dot LEFT to see if it can exit the box from the left side.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- Green dot at [32,20]-[33,21], still inside dark red box [30,18]-[33,21]
- Green dot moved DOWN by 2 rows but blocked at bottom of box
- Green dot seems trapped inside dark red box
- Need to find way to exit box - try LEFT side
Plan: Try moving LEFT to exit box from left side

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move green dot LEFT to try exiting box
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
